# Writing Fixtures

This guide explains how to create and maintain Playwright test fixtures for `@microsoft/fast-html`. Each fixture is a self-contained test case that exercises a specific feature through a live Vite dev server.

## Fixture structure

Every fixture lives in its own subdirectory under `test/fixtures/`:

```
test/fixtures/<feature>/
├── <feature>.spec.ts   # Playwright test
├── entry.html          # Entry template — input to the build step
├── index.html          # Pre-rendered page — GENERATED, do not edit
├── main.ts             # Component definitions and setup
├── state.json          # Initial state for server-side rendering
└── templates.html      # Declarative <f-template> definitions
```

> **Important:** `index.html` is generated by `npm run build:fixtures`. Never edit it by hand — your changes will be overwritten on the next build. If something looks wrong in `index.html`, fix the source files (`entry.html`, `state.json`, `templates.html`) and rebuild.

---

## Creating a new fixture

1. Create a new directory under `test/fixtures/` with a descriptive kebab-case name (e.g., `my-feature/`).
2. Add the five required files described below.
3. Register the fixture in `scripts/build-fixtures.js` by adding the directory name to the `fixtures` array.
4. Run `npm run build:fixtures -w @microsoft/fast-html` to generate `index.html`.
5. Write your Playwright tests in `<feature>.spec.ts`.
6. Run `npm run test:chromium -w @microsoft/fast-html` to verify everything works.

Fixtures are auto-discovered by the Vite config in `../vite.config.ts` — Vite will serve any directory that has an `index.html`. However, the `index.html` is only generated for fixtures listed in `scripts/build-fixtures.js`, so **you must add your fixture name to that file**.

---

## entry.html

The entry file is a minimal HTML document that places your custom elements on the page. It serves as the input to the server-side renderer.

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en-US">
    <head>
        <meta charset="utf-8">
        <title></title>
    </head>
    <body>
        <my-element greeting="{{greeting}}"></my-element>
        <script type="module" src="./main.ts"></script>
    </body>
</html>
```

### Attribute binding guidelines

When writing attribute bindings on root elements in `entry.html`:

| Pattern | Guidance |
|---|---|
| `type="{{type}}"` (primitive same-name) | ✅ Fine — rendered onto the opening tag for `@attr` initialization. |
| `list="{{list}}"` (complex same-name) | ❌ Unnecessary — non-primitive values are stripped from the rendered tag; state propagation provides them automatically. |
| `foo="{{bar}}"` (rename) | ❌ Avoid — rename `bar` to `foo` in `state.json` instead. |
| `:items="{{emptyItems}}"` (property binding) | ✅ Acceptable when multiple instances of the same element need different values for the same property. |

---

## state.json

A JSON object containing the initial state that the server-side renderer uses to evaluate bindings. Property names should match what the templates and entry HTML reference.

```json
{
    "greeting": "Hello",
    "isEnabled": true,
    "items": ["A", "B", "C"]
}
```

Keep `state.json` minimal — only include values that affect the pre-rendered output. Complex objects (arrays, nested data) are supported.

---

## templates.html

This file defines the declarative `<f-template>` elements that describe each custom element's shadow DOM. Each `<f-template>` has a `name` attribute matching the custom element tag name and contains a `<template>` child with the component's markup.

```html
<f-template name="my-element">
    <template>
        <span>{{greeting}}</span>
    </template>
</f-template>
```

### Template syntax reference

| Syntax | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `{{prop}}` | Text/attribute binding | `<span>{{greeting}}</span>` |
| `{{!prop}}` | Negated boolean | `?disabled="{{!isEnabled}}"` |
| `{{a == b}}` | Comparison expression | `?hidden="{{count == 0}}"` |
| `?attr="{{expr}}"` | Boolean attribute | `?disabled="{{!isEnabled}}"` |
| `:prop="{{expr}}"` | Property binding | `:items="{{list}}"` |
| `@event="{handler()}"` | Event binding | `@click="{handleClick()}"` |
| `@event="{handler($e)}"` | Event with event object | `@click="{handleClick($e)}"` |
| `<f-when value="{{expr}}">` | Conditional rendering | See below |
| `<f-repeat value="{{item in list}}">` | List rendering | See below |

#### Conditional rendering (`f-when`)

```html
<f-when value="{{show}}">
    <span>Visible when show is truthy</span>
</f-when>
```

#### List rendering (`f-repeat`)

```html
<f-repeat value="{{item in list}}">
    <li>{{item}}</li>
</f-repeat>
```

The iteration variable (`item`) is scoped to the repeat block. Parent properties remain accessible by name.

Multiple `<f-template>` elements can be defined in a single `templates.html` — one per custom element used in the fixture.

---

## main.ts

The component definition file registers custom elements and configures the template system. Every fixture must:

1. Define element classes extending `FASTElement`.
2. Wrap classes with `RenderableFASTElement()` for server-rendered fixtures that need hydration.
3. Call `TemplateElement.define()` to register the `<f-template>` custom element.

### Basic pattern

```typescript
import { attr, FASTElement, observable } from "@microsoft/fast-element";
import { RenderableFASTElement, TemplateElement } from "@microsoft/fast-html";

class MyElement extends FASTElement {
    @attr
    greeting: string = "Hello";
}
RenderableFASTElement(MyElement).defineAsync({
    name: "my-element",
    templateOptions: "defer-and-hydrate",
});

TemplateElement.define({
    name: "f-template",
});
```

### When to use `@attr` vs `@observable`

- Use `@attr` for values reflected as HTML attributes (strings, booleans) — these can be set in `entry.html`.
- Use `@observable` for internal state or complex values (arrays, objects) — these are provided via `state.json`.

### Exporting types

If your spec file needs to reference element types (e.g., for `page.evaluate` calls), export the classes:

```typescript
export class TestElement extends FASTElement {
    @observable
    list: Array<string> = [];
}
```

Then import in the spec:

```typescript
import type { TestElement } from "./main.js";

await customElement.evaluate((node: TestElement) => {
    node.list = ["A", "B", "C"];
});
```

### Using ObserverMap

For fixtures that test deeply nested property reactivity, configure `TemplateElement` with `observerMap` options:

```typescript
TemplateElement.options({
    "my-element": {
        observerMap: "all",
    },
}).define({
    name: "f-template",
});
```

This enables the `ObserverMap` proxy system to track nested property changes without requiring `@observable` decorators on every property.

---

## Writing the spec file

Spec files use Playwright's test runner. Each fixture's tests navigate to the fixture page and interact with the rendered components.

### Basic structure

```typescript
import { expect, test } from "@playwright/test";

test.describe("my-feature", async () => {
    test("initial render is correct", async ({ page }) => {
        await page.goto("/fixtures/my-feature/");

        const element = page.locator("my-element");
        await expect(element).toHaveText("Hello");
    });

    test("responds to attribute changes", async ({ page }) => {
        await page.goto("/fixtures/my-feature/");

        const element = page.locator("my-element");

        await page.evaluate(() => {
            document
                .querySelector("my-element")!
                .setAttribute("greeting", "World");
        });

        await expect(element).toHaveText("World");
    });
});
```

### Common patterns

- **Navigate to the fixture:** `await page.goto("/fixtures/<feature>/");`
- **Locate elements:** `page.locator("my-element")` or `page.locator("#specific-id")`
- **Modify attributes:** `await page.evaluate(() => { element.setAttribute(...) })`
- **Modify properties:** `await element.evaluate((node: MyElement) => { node.prop = value })`
- **Assert text:** `await expect(element).toHaveText("expected")`
- **Assert attributes:** `await expect(element).toHaveAttribute("attr", "value")`
- **Assert child count:** `await expect(element.locator("li")).toHaveCount(3)`

---

## Rebuilding fixtures

After editing `entry.html`, `templates.html`, or `state.json`, you **must** regenerate `index.html`:

```bash
npm run build:fixtures -w @microsoft/fast-html
```

This runs `scripts/build-fixtures.js`, which:

1. Invokes `@microsoft/fast-build` for each registered fixture with the `--templates`, `--entry`, `--state`, and `--output` flags.
2. Injects the `<f-template>` declarations from `templates.html` before the `<script>` tag in the generated output.
3. Writes the final pre-rendered HTML to `index.html`.

> **Tip:** If you're iterating on a fixture, use `npm run dev -w @microsoft/fast-html` to start the Vite dev server with file watching. But remember to run `build:fixtures` before committing — the Vite dev server uses the generated `index.html`.

---

## Checklist for new fixtures

- [ ] Created fixture directory with all required files
- [ ] Added fixture name to `scripts/build-fixtures.js` `fixtures` array
- [ ] Ran `npm run build:fixtures -w @microsoft/fast-html` to generate `index.html`
- [ ] Wrote Playwright tests in `<feature>.spec.ts`
- [ ] Tests pass locally with `npm run test:chromium -w @microsoft/fast-html`
- [ ] Committed all files **including** the generated `index.html`
